papers.title: Interstellar Bow Shocks around Fast Stars Passing through the Local Interstellar Medium
papers.author_names: J. Michael Shull S. R. Kulkarni

abstract.text: Bow-shocks are produced in the local interstellar medium by the passage of fast stars from the Galactic thin-disk and thick-disk populations with velocities $V_* = $ 40-80 km/s. Stellar transits of local H I clouds occur every 3500-7000 yr on average and last between $10^4$ and $10^5$ yr. There could be 10-20 active bow shocks around low-mass stars inside clouds within 10-15 pc of the Sun. At local cloud distances of 3-10 pc, their turbulent wakes have transverse radial extents $R_{\\\\rm wake} \\\\approx$ 10-300 AU, angular sizes 10-100 arcsec, and Lyman-alpha surface brightnesses of 2-8 Rayleighs in gas with total hydrogen density $n_H \\\\approx 0.1~{\\\\rm cm}^{-3}$ and $V_* =$ 40-80 km/s. These transit wakes may cover an area fraction $f_A \\\\approx (R_{\\\\rm wake}/R_{\\\\rm cl}) \\\\approx 10^{-3}$ of local H I clouds and be detectable in IR (dust), UV (Lya, two-photon), or non-thermal radio emission. Turbulent heating in these wakes could produce the observed elevated rotational populations of H$_2$ ($J \\\\geq 2$) and influence the endothermic formation of CH$^+$ in diffuse interstellar gas at $T > 10^3$ K.

source_fields.sections_text: Introduction: This paper introduces a novel framework for modeling bow shocks from fast stars (40–70 km s⁻¹) traversing warm, magnetized local interstellar clouds, linking shock physics to observed rotational excitation of H₂ and CH⁺ via turbulent heating and non-thermal UV pumping, thereby solving the problem of explaining elevated rotational populations in interstellar gas beyond standard UV pumping mechanisms. Conclusion and Future Prospects: This section concludes that bow shocks from passing stars, particularly low-mass stars, generate detectable turbulent and energetic effects in the local interstellar medium, with observable signatures in infrared, ultraviolet, and radio emissions that can be probed by current and future surveys. Turbulent Heating of the LISM gas: Turbulent heating from bow shocks of fast-moving stars in the local interstellar medium leads to transient, time-dependent temperature increases in LISM clouds, with gas heating to ~20,000–30,000 K and subsequently cooling to ~10⁴ K over timescales comparable to stellar crossing times. Stellar Bow Shocks in the Local ISM: Stellar encounters with warm H I clouds in the Local Interstellar Medium occur at a rate of approximately one collision every 3500–7000 years, leading to frequent bow shock formations, with detectable encounters expected in cloud complexes within 15 pc of the Sun.

title: Interstellar Bow Shocks around Fast Stars Passing through the Local Interstellar Medium

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper presents theoretical modeling of bow shocks from fast stars, discussing statistical probabilities and detectability, but does not present observational data for specific stars.
